title: Cardiopulmonary & Cerebral Resuscitation MCQ – Chapter 41
source: Short Textbook of Anesthesia – Ajay Yadav

questions.question: Cardiac arrest in adults is usually of which origin?
questions.explanation: As per the textbook, cardiac arrest in adults is usually of cardiac origin while in children it is usually of respiratory origin.

questions.question: Unwitnessed cardiac arrest in children should be considered due to which rhythm until proved otherwise?
questions.explanation: Unwitnessed cardiac arrest in children should be considered due to asystole until proved otherwise, while in adults it should be considered due to ventricular fibrillation.

questions.question: Every minute delay in initiating CPR decreases prognosis by what percentage?
questions.explanation: As per AHA chain of survival, every minute delay in CPR decreases prognosis by 7–10%.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Management of CPCR, Page 297

questions.question: The standard CPR sequence was changed from A-B-C to C-A-B in which year's guidelines?
questions.explanation: The standard sequence A (Airway) → B (Breathing) → C (Circulation) was changed to C → A → B in the 2010 CPR guidelines.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Management of CPCR, Page 297

questions.question: According to newer studies, what is the more important cause of airway obstruction in unconscious patients?
questions.explanation: As per newer studies, epiglottis rather than tongue fall has been found to be the more important cause of airway obstruction in unconscious patients.

questions.question: In a patient with suspected cervical spine fracture, what is the preferred method of airway management?
questions.explanation: In patients with suspected cervical spine fracture, head tilt and chin lift should be avoided and airway should be managed only by jaw thrust. However, if airway is not manageable by jaw thrust alone, head tilt and chin lift can be given as life takes priority.

questions.question: Which device, being usable by lay rescuers, is part of Basic Life Support?
questions.explanation: AED can be used by lay rescuers and therefore is part of basic life support. AEDs automatically detect rhythm and deliver shock if rhythm is shockable.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Basic Life Support, Page 298

questions.question: Mouth to mouth rescue breathing is recommended for whom?
questions.explanation: Rescue breath by mouth to mouth is reserved only for trained rescuers (medical as well as non-medical). For untrained lay rescuers, CPR means 'hands only' i.e. only cardiac massage.

questions.question: What is the most definitive method of airway management during CPR?
questions.explanation: Endotracheal intubation is the most definitive and best method for ventilation during CPR.

questions.question: What is the Heimlich maneuver used for?

options.B: Foreign body airway obstruction in adults and older children

questions.explanation: Heimlich maneuver involves abdominal compression 6–10 times with the patient standing and rescuer behind. It is used for adults and older children with foreign body airway obstruction.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Management of Airway Obstruction due to Foreign Body, Page 299

questions.question: For cervical spine fracture patients, which type of neck stabilization is preferred during intubation?
questions.explanation: As intubation often requires removal of cervical collar, manual stabilization is always preferred over collar stabilization.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Airway and Breathing for Patients with Cervical Spine, Page 300

questions.question: During cardiac massage, the sternum should be depressed by how much?

options.A: 2 inches (5 cm) but not more than 2.4 inches (6 cm)

questions.explanation: The force generated during massage should be able to depress sternum by 2 inches (5 cm) but not more than 2.4 inches (6 cm) to avoid complications due to excessive pressure.

questions.question: What is the recommended rate of chest compressions during CPR?
questions.explanation: The rate of compression should be 100–120 compressions/minute but not more than 120, because at rates >120/min, compression depth decreases significantly.

questions.question: Effective cardiac output generated by successful cardiac massage is what percentage of normal?
questions.explanation: Effective cardiac output generated by successful massage is only 30% of normal, therefore all efforts should be directed to establish spontaneous circulation.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Physiological Considerations of Cardiac Massage, Page 301

questions.question: What is the compression to ventilation ratio for adults WITHOUT advanced airway, irrespective of the number of rescuers?
questions.explanation: Without advanced airway, the ratio for adults should be 30:2 (30 compressions followed by 2 breaths) irrespective of number of resuscitators.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Compression to Ventilation Ratio, Page 301

questions.question: With advanced airway in place, what is the ventilation rate during CPR?
questions.explanation: With advanced airway, breathing is at a rate of 10 breaths/minute (1 breath every 6 seconds) without any synchronization with compressions.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Compression to Ventilation Ratio, Page 301

questions.question: What is the minimum ETCO2 that should be produced by successful cardiac massage?
questions.explanation: Successful cardiac massage should be able to produce ETCO2 of at least 20 mm Hg. If ETCO2 is less than 10 mm Hg, cardiac massage should be considered grossly abnormal.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Monitoring of CPR, Page 301

questions.question: ETCO2 becoming normal (40 mm Hg) during CPR is the earliest indicator of what?
questions.explanation: ETCO2 becoming normal (40 mm Hg) is the earliest indicator of return of normal circulation during CPR.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Monitoring of CPR, Page 301

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a shockable rhythm?
questions.explanation: Shockable rhythms include VF, pulseless VT and polymorphic VT (Torsade de Pointes). Non-shockable rhythms include asystole and pulseless electrical activity (PEA).
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Management of Arrhythmias Seen During Cardiac Arrest, Page 301

questions.question: What is the recommended energy for monophasic defibrillation?
questions.explanation: With monophasic defibrillators, all shocks should be of 360 Joules. With biphasic defibrillators, 150–200 J (exponential waveform) or 120 J (rectilinear waveform).

questions.question: What is the recommended defibrillation paddle size for adults?
questions.explanation: Paddle sizes: Adults – 13 cm, Children – 8 cm, Infants – 4.5 cm.

questions.question: After delivering a defibrillation shock, what should be done immediately?

options.B: Give 5 cycles of 30:2 or 2 minutes of CPR before checking rhythm

questions.explanation: Immediately after giving shock, give 5 cycles of 30:2 (without advanced airway) or 2 minutes of CPR (with advanced airway) before checking rhythm, as it takes ~2 minutes for heart to generate cardiac output even if rhythm becomes sinus.

questions.question: In monitored in-hospital patients, defibrillation should ideally be done within how many minutes?
questions.explanation: Ideally, in monitored patients, defibrillation should be done within 3 minutes.

questions.question: What is the most common cause of pulseless electrical activity (PEA)?
questions.explanation: Hypovolemia (shock) is the most common cause of PEA as stated in the textbook.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Pulseless Electrical Activity (PEA), Page 303

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT one of the 5H causes of PEA?
questions.explanation: The 5H causes of PEA are: Hypovolemia, Hypokalemia/Hyperkalemia, Hypothermia, Hypoxia, and Hydrogen ion (acidosis). Hypernatremia is not included.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Causes of PEA, Page 304

questions.question: Which of the following is one of the 5T causes of PEA?
questions.explanation: The 5T causes of PEA are: Tension Pneumothorax, Tamponade (cardiac), Thrombosis (coronary/pulmonary), Tablets (drug overdoses)/Toxins, and Trauma.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Causes of PEA, Page 304

questions.question: Asystole is described as:

options.A: The terminal event of all arrhythmias with very poor prognosis
options.B: The most common initial rhythm in adults
options.C: The rhythm most responsive to adrenaline

questions.explanation: Asystole is the terminal event of all arrhythmias. The prognosis of asystole is very poor as compared to PEA and ventricular fibrillation.

questions.question: Cough CPR is applicable in which situation?
questions.explanation: Cough CPR is applicable to a conscious patient having VF. Coughing during VF increases intrathoracic pressure and can maintain cerebral perfusion (consciousness) for 90 seconds.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Newer Techniques/Variations in CPR, Page 305

questions.question: What is the target core body temperature range in targeted temperature management (TTM) after cardiac arrest?
questions.explanation: TTM means maintaining core body temperature between 32–36°C for 24 hours. This was changed from the 2010 guideline of 32–34°C for 12 hours.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Postcardiac Arrest Care, Page 305

questions.question: What is the target SpO2 range in post-cardiac arrest care?
questions.explanation: Hyperoxia can produce complications including ARDS, therefore use minimum FiO2 which maintains target SpO2 between 94 and 98%.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Postcardiac Arrest Care, Page 305

questions.question: What blood sugar range (mg/dL) should be maintained in post-resuscitation care?
questions.explanation: Moderate blood sugar of 144–180 mg/dL (8–10 mmol) should be maintained in the post-resuscitation period, as both hypoglycemia and hyperglycemia are unacceptable.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Postcardiac Arrest Care, Page 305

questions.question: Emergency PCI for STEMI patients after cardiac arrest should preferably be performed within what time?
questions.explanation: Emergency (preferably within 90 minutes) coronary angiography/angioplasty is recommended for all patients with STEMI.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Postcardiac Arrest Care, Page 305

questions.question: When should neurologic prognostication be performed after ROSC in patients NOT given TTM?
questions.explanation: Prognostication is recommended 72 hours after return of spontaneous circulation for those who have not been given TTM.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Postcardiac Arrest Care, Page 305

questions.question: From a CPR point of view, what age group defines an 'infant'?
questions.explanation: From CPR point of view: Neonate = first 4 weeks, Infant = 4 weeks to 1 year, Child = 1 year till puberty, Adult = beyond puberty.

questions.question: What is the compression to ventilation ratio for a CHILD with TWO rescuers and NO advanced airway?
questions.explanation: For children without advanced airway, the ratio is 30:2 for one rescuer and 15:2 for two rescuers (while for adults it is 30:2 irrespective of rescuers).

questions.question: What is the pulse check site for infants during CPR?
questions.explanation: Pulse check sites: Infants – Brachial/Femoral, Children – Carotid, Adults – Carotid/Femoral.

questions.question: What heart rate in a pediatric patient with signs of poor perfusion is an indication to start chest compressions?
questions.explanation: Heart rate <60/min with signs of poor perfusion (pallor/cyanosis) is the indication to start chest compressions in the pediatric age group.

questions.question: What is the first shock energy for pediatric manual defibrillation?
questions.explanation: Energy selection for shock (manual) in pediatric patients is 2 J/kg for first shock and 4 J/kg subsequently.

questions.question: What is the compression to ventilation ratio for NEWBORN resuscitation?
questions.explanation: Compression ventilation ratio for newborns is 3:1 (90 compressions with 30 breaths) and is synchronized.

questions.question: What is the indication for starting chest compressions in a newborn?

options.A: HR <60/min despite adequate ventilation with 100% O2 for 30 seconds

questions.explanation: In newborns, indication for chest compression is HR <60/min in spite of adequate ventilation with 100% oxygen for 30 seconds.

questions.question: During CPR in pregnancy, how should aortocaval compression be relieved?
questions.explanation: Lateral tilt has been eliminated from new CPR guidelines as it compromises CPR quality. Aortocaval compression should be achieved by uterine displacement.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: CPR in Pregnancy, Page 307

questions.question: Emergency cesarean section during maternal cardiac arrest should be considered if fetus is >25 weeks and can be performed within how many minutes?
questions.explanation: Emergency LSCS should be considered if fetus >25 weeks and LSCS can be performed within 4 minutes of maternal arrest.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: CPR in Pregnancy, Page 307

questions.question: What is the fluid of choice during CPR?
questions.explanation: Normal saline is the fluid of choice during CPR. Glucose-containing solutions increase cerebral edema, and Ringer lactate should be avoided as calcium present can cause neuronal injury.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Fluid During CPR, Page 307

questions.question: What is the dose of adrenaline by endotracheal route compared to intravenous dose?
questions.explanation: The dose by endotracheal route is 2.5 times of intravenous dose and the drug should be diluted with 10 mL of saline in adults and 5 mL in children.

questions.question: Which of the following drugs can NOT be administered via endotracheal route?
questions.explanation: Drugs that can be delivered by ET route are: Adrenaline, Atropine, Lignocaine (Xylocard), and Naloxone. Amiodarone is not in this list.

questions.question: Routine use of sodium bicarbonate without proven acidosis during CPR is harmful because of all EXCEPT:

options.A: Is hypertonic and increases plasma osmolarity
options.C: Produces CO2 which can worsen neuronal injury

questions.explanation: The textbook lists harms of sodium bicarbonate as: CO2 production worsening neuronal injury, increased intracranial tension, paradoxical intracellular acidosis, and hypertonic effect increasing plasma osmolarity. Direct myocardial depression is not mentioned.
questions.reference: Ch 41, Section: Use of Sodium Bicarbonate, Page 308

questions.question: The only recommended modality for global cerebral ischemia during CPR is:
questions.explanation: The only recommended modality for global cerebral ischemia seen in CPR is targeted temperature management. Barbiturates, Propofol, and calcium channel blockers are useful for focal ischemia but their role in global ischemia is not proven.

questions.question: In the criteria for brain death, no spontaneous respiration should occur even if pCO2 increases to:
questions.explanation: One of the clinical tests for brain death is no spontaneous respiration even if pCO2 increases to 60 mm Hg.

questions.question: What is the IV dose of adrenaline during CPR and how frequently is it repeated?
questions.explanation: The CPR dose of adrenaline is 1 mg IV to be repeated every 3–5 minutes in concentration of 1:10,000.

questions.question: What is the first dose of amiodarone during CPR for ventricular tachycardia?
questions.explanation: Amiodarone is the drug of choice for ventricular tachycardia. The first dose is 300 mg IV and subsequent doses are 150 mg.
